critique.summary: The video titled 'It Took Me 30 Years to Solve This VFX Problem – Green Screen Problem' by Corridor Crew discusses longstanding challenges in visual effects related to green screen compositing and presents a new approach using neural networks and procedural generation to address these issues. The video explores the transparency and chroma key problems in green screen technology and showcases initial results of the proposed solution.
mainPoints: Green screens have inherent problems, particularly with transparency and chroma keying.
Traditional methods have not fully solved these issues over the past decades.
The video introduces a novel approach involving neural networks and procedural generation to improve green screen compositing.
Initial results of this new method are demonstrated, suggesting potential advancements in VFX technology.
critique.truthfulness: The claims about the challenges of green screen compositing and the use of neural networks for improvement align with known issues and recent trends in visual effects technology. Corridor Crew is a recognized content creator in the VFX community, and their discussion reflects real technical challenges and innovations. However, the video appears to be a personal or team project presentation rather than a peer-reviewed or independently verified scientific breakthrough. The information is accurate in describing the problem and the approach but should be considered as a demonstration of ongoing work rather than a definitive solution.
considerations: The video does not provide independent verification or peer-reviewed evidence of the effectiveness of the new method.
It lacks multiple perspectives or expert commentary outside the Corridor Crew team.
The solution's scalability, cost, and integration into existing VFX pipelines are not discussed.
Potential limitations or challenges of the neural network approach are not detailed.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around advancements in green screen technology, AI-driven keying methods, and practical production challenges. Participants analyze technical innovations, industry implications, and personal experiences with green screen workflows.

comments.author: lynnharry
comments.text: It’s fascinating to see the bridge between academic research and industry application here. While Image Matting is a massive research area in Computer Vision, academia often focuses on solving perfect 'benchmarks.' Corridor Crew effectively took that foundational research, like neural unmixing and synthetic training, and adapted it to solve the 'messy' reality of production, like tracking markers and motion blur. It’s a great example of using open-source deep learning resources to build a tool that prioritized workflow over just a high accuracy score.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a nuanced view of how research translates into practical tools, highlighting the importance of adapting academic work for real-world use.

comments.author: dylan604
comments.text:
The sad thing about this is the problems encountered during post from the production team saying "fix it post" during the shoot. I've been on set for green screen shoots where the lighting was not done properly. I watched the gaffer walk across the set taking readings from his meter before saying the lighting was good. I flip on the waveform and told him it was not even (which never goes down well when camera dept tells the gaffer it's not right). He put up an argument, went back and took measurements again before repeating it was good. I flipped the screen around and showed him where it was obviously not even. A third set of meter readings and he starts adjust lights. Once the footage was in post, the fx team commented about how easy the keys were because of the even lighting.

The problem is that the vast majority of people on set have no clue what is going on in post. To the point, when the budget is big enough, a post supervisor is present on production days to give input so "fixing it in post" is minimized. When there is no budget, you'll see situations just like in the first 30 seconds of TFA's video. A single lamp lighting the background so you can easily see the light falling off and the shadows from wrinkles where the screen was just pulled out of the bag 10 minutes before shooting. People just don't realize how much light a green screen takes. They also fail to have enough space so they can pull the talent far enough off the wall to avoid the green reflecting back onto the talent's skin.

TL;DR They solved something to make post less expensive because they cut corners during production.
comments.analysis: This comment emphasizes real-world production issues, illustrating how poor on-set practices complicate post-production, which the technology aims to address.

comments.author: qingcharles
comments.text:
I use the Adobe version of this in Photoshop every day and I assumed that Adobe solved this the same way, but used professionals to cut out the subjects from the backgrounds then fed both versions into their AI.

Since they added it a year or so ago it has been game-changing. I'm cutting out portraits every day and having a magical tool that cuts out the subject with perfect hair cut out with a single click is sci-fi.

Here's a demo of Photoshop's tool:

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=SNVJN6PKeGQ

(the other magical Photoshop tool is the one that removes reflections from windows, which is even more insane when you reverse it and tell it you only want the reflection and not what's on the other side of the glass)
comments.analysis: This comment shares practical insight into industry workflows, comparing AI-assisted tools with traditional manual methods, adding context to the technological discussion.

comments.author: Springtime
comments.text: In an earlier video they made a couple years back about Disney's sodium vapor technique Paul Debevec suggested he was considering creating a dataset using a similar premise: filming enough perfectly masked references to be able to train models to achieve better keying. So it was interesting seeing Corridor tackle this by instead using synthetic data.
comments.analysis: This comment discusses different research strategies, providing context on how synthetic data is used to improve keying techniques.

comments.author: diacritical
comments.text:
From ~04:10 till 05:00 they talk about sodium-vapor lights and how Disney has the exclusive rights to use it. From what I read the knowledge on how to make them is a trade secret, so it's not patented. Seems weird that it would be hard to recreate something from the 1950's.

I also wonder how many hours were wasted by people who had to use inferior technology because Disney kept it secret. Cutting out animals and objects from the background 1 frame at a time seems so mindnumbingly boring.
comments.analysis: This comment highlights industry secrecy and its impact on technological development, providing historical and practical context.
